paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 22: The Frog at MacRitchie (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 1 girl on a school excursion to the MacRitchie Treetop Walk who finds a tiny green frog on the suspension bridge, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [10 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format, pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 6 short-answer items (Q1–Q6) and a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q7) are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
On a Tuesday in March, our Sec 1 class went to MacRitchie Reservoir for Geography fieldwork. Before the walk, Mrs Tan, our form teacher, gathered us under a Tembusu tree and reminded us that the forest was a home. 'This is the frog's house,' she said, 'not ours. The trees, the lizards, the spiders — all of this is theirs. We are only visitors today.'

Paragraph 2
My two best friends, Priya and Yi Xin, walked on either side of me. We had been put into groups of three. By the time we reached the suspension bridge of the Treetop Walk, my water bottle was almost empty. The bridge swayed a little.

Paragraph 3
Halfway across, Priya stopped so suddenly that Yi Xin bumped into her back. Priya pointed at the wooden railing. There, sitting completely still, was the tiniest frog I had ever seen. It was bright green, no bigger than my thumbnail. Its eyes were like two small black dots. It seemed to think that if it stayed very still, we would not see it.

Paragraph 4
Yi Xin pulled out her empty water bottle. 'We can put it inside,' she whispered. 'Take it back to school.' Priya nodded. She had already cupped her hand near the frog, ready to scoop it up. The frog still did not move.

Paragraph 5
I did not say anything. I thought about Mrs Tan under the Tembusu tree. 'This is the frog's house,' she had said, 'not ours.' I looked at the bottle in Yi Xin's hand. I looked at the frog. I looked at the long way back to the bus.

Paragraph 6
'No,' I said. My voice was small but clear. 'We can't take it. Mrs Tan said this is the frog's house. If we put it in the bottle, we are stealing from its house.' Yi Xin made a face. Priya rolled her eyes. 'Aiyoh, Ling, it's just one frog,' she said. But she did not move her hand any closer.

Paragraph 7
Nobody spoke. Then Yi Xin slowly put the water bottle back into her bag. Priya found a wide green leaf on the railing and gently nudged the frog onto it. The frog blinked once and hopped into the bushes below. None of us spoke after that.

Paragraph 8
On the bus back to school, the air conditioning hummed and most of the class fell asleep. Yi Xin sat next to me, looking out of the window. Without turning her head, she said quietly, 'Ling. You were right about the frog.' I did not say anything. I leaned my forehead against the cool glass and looked at the trees passing by.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past examination paper. MacRitchie: a large reservoir and nature reserve in central Singapore; Treetop Walk: a free-standing suspension bridge through the forest canopy; Tembusu: a large native Singaporean tree; aiyoh: a Singlish exclamation expressing mild frustration.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what did Mrs Tan mean when she said 'This is the frog's house, not ours'? [1]
questions.answer: she meant that the forest belongs to the animals that live in it, and the class were only visitors who should respect that and not take or disturb anything

questions.stem: Q2. In Paragraph 3, what is the effect of describing the frog as 'no bigger than my thumbnail' with eyes 'like two small black dots'? [1]
questions.answer: the comparisons make the frog seem extremely tiny and delicate, almost like a toy — this helps the reader understand why the friends were tempted to keep it but also how vulnerable it really was

questions.stem: Q3. Why does the narrator say the frog 'seemed to think that if it stayed very still, we would not see it' (Paragraph 3)? [1]
questions.answer: she imagines the frog is using stillness as a defence against being noticed by predators or humans — this gives the frog a small personality and reminds the reader that it is a living creature trying to survive

questions.stem: Q4. In Paragraph 5, why does the narrator look 'at the long way back to the bus' before deciding what to say? [1]
questions.answer: she is weighing up how far they would have to carry the frog, which makes her realise that keeping it in a bottle would be a long and unkind journey for such a small creature, helping her make up her mind

questions.stem: Q5. What is suggested by the detail that Priya 'did not move her hand any closer' after rolling her eyes (Paragraph 6)? [1]
questions.answer: even though Priya pretended not to agree with the narrator, in her actions she had already accepted the narrator's point — she was no longer trying to catch the frog, which shows that part of her knew the narrator was right

questions.stem: Q6. What does Yi Xin's quiet remark on the bus, 'Ling. You were right about the frog' (Paragraph 8), reveal about how she has changed? [1]
questions.answer: Yi Xin has had time to think about what happened on the bridge and now privately admits that the narrator's decision was the correct one — she has moved from wanting to keep the frog to respecting the choice to let it go

sections.instruction: Q7. The narrator's main feeling shifts as the excursion at MacRitchie unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: curious / torn / resolute / quietly proud / bored / scared / annoyed / surprised. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Frog at MacRitchie' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift through the afternoon — from curious as she crouches down to look at the tiny green frog on the railing, to torn as Yi Xin pulls out the empty water bottle and Priya cups her hand to scoop it up, to resolute when she says clearly that they cannot take it, ending quietly proud on the bus ride home when Yi Xin tells her she was right. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q7(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 as she crouches down to look at the bright green frog sitting motionless on the wooden railing? [1]

Q7(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 4–5 as Yi Xin pulls out the empty water bottle, Priya cups her hand near the frog, and the narrator thinks back to Mrs Tan under the Tembusu tree? [1]
Q7(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6 as she says, in a small but clear voice, 'No. We can't take it. Mrs Tan said this is the frog's house'? [1]

Q7(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 on the bus ride back to school, leaning her forehead against the cool glass after Yi Xin quietly tells her she was right about the frog? [1]
